item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to be honest, I'm usually not uptight about image changes, but the new -2 image is very obviously just a shaken up Sphynx cat.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The images that were used in this article have been replaced with the following:

Filename: scp-019-new.jpg
Name: Hydria. 4th cent. B.C.jpg
Author: George E. Koronaios
License: CC BY-SA 4.0
Source Link: Wikimedia Commons

Filename: scp-019-2-new.jpg
Author: Elogee FishTruck, Dmitry Makeev
License: CC BY-SA 3.0
Source Link: SCP Foundation Wiki
Derivative of:

Name: Sphynx kittens.jpg
Author: Dmitry Makeev
License: CC BY-SA 4.0
Source Link: Wikimedia Commons

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So what is -19-1?

There’s a -2, but no -1?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that the first image appears to be from the British Museum, which would be copyrighted if it was from the museum's website, and no indication has been given that the owner of the photo (if taken by someone else) has released the photo under a license compliant with the wiki's (CC BY-SA 3.0).

The second image's license was unable to be determined, and has been assumed to be non-compliant. As such, both have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one, mostly because I imagine the little runts floating around helplessly in a black void, all the time looking up at the opening of the vase for light, climbing over each other.
They're thinking… 'God I hope someone tips this stupid thing over.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this article quite a bit - the picture of the 019-2 specimen is nicely creepy as a result of the human-like face, and it's made worse by the idea that these things are basically just angry balls of meat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If a successful method is discovered, SCP-019 is to be destroyed with prejudice.

Man this whole sentence just really rubs me the wrong way. I mean, there’s a really great idea here but the tone throughout the article just keeps throwing me off (SCP-019-2 are “surprisingly” flammable?).

But the idea’s just too good for me to downvote. Gotta keep it at neutral here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff notice: this user has been permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: huh i will never see pots the same again

item.title: OK.
item.content:encoded:
Looking back at it, this is definitely a product of its time.

I mean, if these things die after 48 hours…why not just stick the pot inside a massive vault made of concrete/water/molten lava/titanium/telekill/big lizard guts/coldposted mary-sues/the hopes and dreams of senior researcher L███ and wait for the fucks to die themselves?

Okay, jokey-jokes aside. It would make more sense to just lock this up super secure and let these things die on their own. But I know that this is a product of its time, and this oversight is not gonna stop me from upvoting this.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't we label the monsters SCP-019-1 then?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: This is too big of an edit for a non-author to make. You'd have to get approval from the author before making such changes. I've reverted the changes, as such.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've made the edits very confusing. You've missed some, to start with, and secondly, the main SCP object should be SCP-019, not SCP-019-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made a few small edits. The article designated an SCP-019-2 without ever designating an SCP-019-1 so I just labeled the pot itself as SCP-019-1. Also, I changed anything referring to the pot itself to say SCP-019-1 rather than SCP-019.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice, another SCP marked for termination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I first saw the picture I was frightened. They don't look as bad now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…What if we put a lid on it? Sure, they're strong, but they aren't strong enough to break out of, say, an iron box. A very small iron box. What is the point of these containment procedures? It never says anywhere that the little buggers can break through any other object, so I don't see why they need to waste so many resources on these guys. It seems like it should be Safe.

And, because it is an early article on the site, it suffers from "thing what must be destroyed", and a few clinical tone breaks, such as "destroyed with prejudice". Of course, I can forgive it a little for being an old article. I can also forgive it more because it's just a good idea for an SCP, and the rest of the article that I have not touched upon is very well written.

Neutral-Vote. Although I understand that this article is never being changed, I still wanted to explain my neutral-vote.

EDIT: Upon a re-read, I don't even like the rest of the article. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does anyone else think that the SCP-019-2 instance in the picture looks kind of ugly-cute? It looks like an adorable sphynx gollum-kitten.
Yay? LOL

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd never realized that before. I'm pretty sure that's what the picture is of. Good eye!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like a Sphynx

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the dilly dally is that second image supposed to BE. Its wierding me out, man. Normally, images don't bother me too much, but what is it????? Mystery.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Look even with the adapting creatures, this thing should still be Euclid, it's still not difficult to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's just Pandora's Box. There's really not enough deviation from the original myth to make it interesting. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: agree

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only one glaring flaw in this article:

If a successful method is discovered, SCP-019 is to be destroyed with prejudice.

Not only is this contrary to Foundation standard operating procedure, but in this specific instance the potential consequences for such an action are so blatant that even the GOC might think twice about it.

Do we really want to smash Pandora's Box?
one hundred is best hundred except for nine hundred

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good take on Pandora's Box; just enough deviation from the legend to make it seem more plausible, while still retaining the core mechanics. Arguably we're loosing the moral of the story, but in the model that was the part that was added to make the legend.

item.title: Re: question
item.content:encoded: Oh, wow. How the hell did I miss the part where theyre adapting to the flamethrowers? Shit, okay, yeah, now the Keter designation makes sense. Teacup 682s (o-o)"

item.title: Re: question
item.content:encoded: Right, because it's difficult to contain.

item.title: Re: question
item.content:encoded:
A nuke that we have the launch code, yes. A nuke that is armed and some one that we don't know has the launch code for is keter.

Actually, I'm on a phone so let me keep this brief. The object class should represent how the object needs to be handled and secured. Do you want to keep something that could end all life in the safe objects wing just because you can toss it in a box?

That's a convo for the forums though. He's wrong on the jar because the creatures are adapting and will always need constant monitoring. It's actually not easily contained at all.

item.title: Re: question
item.content:encoded:
Safe Class objects are easily contained, and/or pose little to no threat to human life. Safe class does not automatically mean harmless; instead, it means that we can lock them up and not have to worry about them.

Keter Class SCP objects are objects that require extremely specific Containment Procedures, and are so inhumanly dangerous that they must be contained at all costs. Alternatively, Keter Class objects are simply so difficult to contain effectively that they need higher levels of care and resources to suppress.

Vincent is correct. "A nuke is safe", and so on.

item.title: Re: question
item.content:encoded: Reread the object classes. The potential results of a breach determines keter status, not how easy an item is to contain.

item.title: Re: question
item.content:encoded:
The jar itself is the -1, if I'm not mistaken.

Edit: a question I have, though, is how something this easy to control is considered Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh heh…surprisingly flammable! I like it. +1

item.title: question
item.content:encoded: What is SCP-019-1? The entry skipped that number, and went straight for SCP-019-2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just thought I'd throw out that SCPs like this are what make me love this site. Just the idea that something is continually getting stronger and stronger, and one day will probably overwhelm the site within which it is located. I love articles that give readers a look at how the Foundation's struggles are ultimately futile - if this thing is evolving, theoretically it will one day spawn an entity that cannot be destroyed by anything the Foundation has in its arsenal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey Light, I just wanted to tell you how well this is written. It was about the 3rd SCP I ever read and is still one of my favorites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops. I should probably look at the age of an article before I suggest mass revisions. Disregard please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally think this article is fine as it is. We don't need to go back and edit every single older scp that doesn't have todays super dry tone. Up to Light of course, but again, i think its great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, I'm pretty new here, but from my limited experience, I think certain parts of this article don't match foundation tone, and I'd like to offer my services as a copy-editor.

"They often choose to attack with teeth or claws. Although fairly delicate (also, surprisingly, flammable), they are reasonably strong and pose a considerable threat in large numbers."

1. I suggest "They often attack with…" or better yet "They attack with…"
2. The parenthetical remark sounds conversational and thus deviates from the 'clinical, detached' tone of SCP articles. Also, since the vulnerability to fire seems to be important in the Addendum, it probably shouldn't be in parentheses anyway. I suggest "Specimens/subjects/entities are delicate and flammable, but are strong enough to pose a serious threat in large numbers.

"In addition, tipping or tilting SCP-019 will create a reaction as though it was previously “filled” with SCP-019-2 specimens"

This sounds a little awkward, how about "When tipped or tilted, SCP-019 will react as though it was/were filled with SCP-019-2 specimens" - still sounds a little odd though.

"The specimen only lived for under 48 hours, and a dissection revealed anatomy consistent on a cellular level with normal biology, but with an extremely unstable musculoskeletal structure. Other notable anomalies included an unstable respiratory system, next to no digestive tract, and virtually no other internal organs. All other captured specimens have followed similar patterns of behavior and demise."

1. Sounds a little dramatic, suggest "The specimen survived for approximately 48 hours (or some more specific number).
2. "but with an unstable musculoskeletal structure.
3. "an incomplete digestive tract"
4 "few additional internal organs" or "an absence of normal internal organs"

I'm not sure if these are the sorts of edits I need permission to make, so I'll post this here. Aside from those issues the tone of the article looked fine to me. I like to concept, especially the idea that the specimens might originally have been citizens of Ancient Greece. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like to think (for bonus creepiness) that they will eventually become resistant to pretty much everything, break loose and wreck havoc.

If “it” is indeed sentient I would bet that whatever is responsible for this would recognize the rotation pattern and simply stack up the resistances.

But that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing with super bugs is that their adaptations were actually genes already present in the bacteria, but the changes that made them more resistant to antibiotics make the bacteria "less fit" than non-resistant bacteria. With as many antibiotics we throw on pretty much everything, the resistance becomes worth it and becomes a dominant trait. In theory if we stopped using antibiotics for a decade, super bugs would get wiped out back to pre-antibiotic levels.. (Of course, the effects of using no antibiotics makes this a last resort option.)

What this translates to, is how long would whatever agency is making these things fire resistant keep it up if the Foundation started using an acid bath? Could they use a rotation of extermination devices to keep them under control?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What really gets me in this one is how it seems to be adapting to the containment procedure.

Makes me wonder if the foundation will end up creating some kind of highly resistant SCPs, just like a superbug or super bacterium.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man I really like this one the more I think about it. What really gets me is how human-like these things are. Makes you wonder…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not much, I think. They are sufficiently different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, we've already got a jar that produces creatures vulnerable to incineration? What does this mean for SCP-244?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely one of my inspirations when writing this, actually. Heh.
The other of which, definitely was not that endless pot of Miniblins in that island in Windwaker. No. Definitely not. No way. Completely unrelated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The original inspiration behind the myth of Pandora's Box, perhaps. The original was a jar, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally think if you try to add any more, it'd cross the line into too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand that not everything is needed, just throwing out some ideas that I had. Like I said, I do like this piece, it just seems to need a little more, you know? Like that since the 019-2 are/were vulnerable to fire, it could be that the Burning of Rome occurred to control an infestation.
I don't want to see all my questions answered because that takes away part of the charm, like if we knew how 343 did what he does, he would stop being so intriguing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too much expansion leads to cluttered SCPs that detract from the original intent. These are synopses of the logs, we don't need EVERYTHING in them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, just seems that it can be expanded a bit. Such as:
Consulting with a historian of the possible time period and determining if the vase has any markings that hint to the time, symbolic meaning or anything of that sort.
Recording the sounds of 019-2 and translating to see if anything is said.
What happens if the room is put below 0 Celsius? Will this retard the production of 019-2?
What happens if the room is raised above 0 Celsius? Will this speed the production of 019-2?
Is 019-2 now fire-proof or just harder to light on fire? I.E. do we need a hotter production of flame to eliminate them?

Just the first things that popped into my head and thought I would share. Don't want to create an experiment log if you have plans for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture of the critter is freakin' me out, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it… done and done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: myself, i like it. it's not so much a SCP-maker…the critters just seem to be it's effect, such as it is. I would make a small tweak, however, showing that the critters lack insides. Not just reproductive, but next to no digestive system, limited respiratory…basically like mayflies. Horrible, horrible mayflies, a bundle of nerves, muscles, and sharp bits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm… it's pretty similar to SCP-354, not to mention that SCPs that make other SCPs are on the Big List of Cliches. Still, despite the unfortunate subject matter, this is a very well-written and well-presented SCP. Good job!
